rationale_for_baseline_tier: A known type 1 diabetic with an off-the-scale home glucose, relentless vomiting, litres of fluid loss, deep rapid (Kussmaul) breathing, a ketotic breath odour and progressive drowsiness is in a severe ketoacidotic crisis with impending hypovolaemic shock and reduced conscious level. This is an immediate threat to life needing IV fluid resuscitation and monitored insulin — T5 (call 000, emergency ambulance) is the only appropriate tier. Delay to a GP or self-care would risk cardiovascular collapse, cerebral oedema and death.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Off-scale home glucose with deep rapid breathing, relentless vomiting and increasing drowsiness in a type 1 diabetic
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Call 000 immediately — within minutes; do not wait for a GP appointment
escalation_edges.rationale: This constellation is a diabetic emergency requiring hospital fluids and insulin; every hour of delay worsens the acidosis and dehydration.
escalation_edges.communication_template: This is an emergency. I'm going to ask you to call 000 for an ambulance right now and tell them she is a type 1 diabetic who is very unwell with a sky-high blood sugar, vomiting and deep, fast breathing.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Increasing drowsiness / becoming difficult to rouse before the ambulance arrives
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Stay on the line with 000; place her on her side in case she vomits
escalation_edges.rationale: A falling conscious level threatens the airway (aspiration risk with vomiting) and signals dangerous progression — paramedics must be updated immediately.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she gets harder to wake, roll her onto her side so she can't choke if she's sick, and tell 000 straight away that she's becoming very drowsy.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Becomes completely unresponsive and stops breathing normally
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Begin CPR immediately and continue until paramedics take over
escalation_edges.rationale: Cardiorespiratory arrest is the terminal event of untreated DKA; bystander CPR under 000 dispatcher guidance is the only intervention available at home.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If she becomes completely unresponsive and isn't breathing normally, tell 000 immediately — they will talk you through starting CPR, and you should begin chest compressions straight away.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Adolescent with type 1 diabetes; parent present and able to act on instructions
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Direct instructions to the parent as the acting carer — make the 000 call, position and monitor the patient, gather insulin pens and the glucose logbook for the paramedics. No home drug dose is calculated.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Parent is frightened and managing a rapidly deteriorating child alone
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Keep instructions short, calm and sequential; confirm the ambulance is called first, then positioning and monitoring, and reassure that staying on the line with 000 is appropriate.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 for an ambulance now and say she is a type 1 diabetic who is very unwell — sky-high blood sugar, vomiting, deep fast breathing and getting drowsy. While you wait, lie her flat with her legs raised, keep her on her side if she is sick, watch her breathing and how awake she is, and do NOT give a big insulin dose or make her drink large amounts. Unlock the door for the paramedics and gather her insulin pens and sugar logbook.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately for an emergency ambulance
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours or a GP appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: The hospital will provide a discharge summary and an updated diabetes sick-day action plan; take these to the GP and diabetes team, and arrange endocrinology follow-up after discharge.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the presentation as gastroenteritis or a manageable high-sugar day and advising self-care or a GP appointment.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Untreated ketoacidosis progresses to hypovolaemic shock, cerebral oedema, coma and death while care is delayed.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to the emergency department rather than an ambulance in a drowsy, vomiting, hypovolaemic patient — deterioration or airway compromise en route without paramedic monitoring, IV access or resuscitation capability.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: T5 is correct here, so there is effectively no over-triage concern; calling 000 for this presentation is appropriate, not excessive.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: The exact glucose, ketone and acid-base values cannot be known via telehealth — the meter only shows 'HI'.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat a known type 1 diabetic who is this unwell as a diabetic emergency and escalate to 000; do not wait for numbers that can only be obtained in hospital.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Delaying escalation to 'see if she improves', requesting a repeat home glucose reading, or attributing symptoms to a viral illness because the exact biochemistry is unconfirmed.
